sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 172, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: SPACE had no effect - board unchanged. Player at (25,32)-(27,35), above blue bar. The blue bar shows a light section where player is horizontally aligned, suggesting visual feedback. Hypothesis: player must OVERLAP with objects (not just be adjacent) to collect them. Need to move DOWN to overlap with the blue bar.
- Plan: Move RIGHT to collect the remaining bar, then collect the other blue squares.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 171 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 2.4x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
After moving RIGHT, the player moved from cols 33-35 to cols 36-38. The blue bar was at cols 36-39 with 10 pixels, and now the player is at cols 36-38. The blue bar now shows as two separate blue objects: one at [[28, 32], [28, 35]] (4 pixels) and another at [[31, 32], [31, 35]] (4 pixels). The player is at [[28, 36], [28, 38], [31, 38], [31, 36]] with 12 pixels.
Wait, looking at the segmentation more carefully:
- Blue at [[28, 32], [28, 35]] - 4 pixels (top bar segment)
- Blue at [[31, 32], [31, 35]] - 4 pixels (bottom bar segment)
- Player at [[28, 36], [28, 38], [31, 38], [31, 36]] - 12 pixels
So the blue bar was at cols 32-35 (not 36-39 as I thought before). The player is now at cols 36-38, to the RIGHT of the bar. The bar seems to have split into two parts (top and bottom).
Looking at the images, the green player has moved right and is now overlapping with the blue bar. The blue bar appears to have been partially collected - the left part is gone and only the right part remains (or vice versa).
Wait, let me re-examine. Before the RIGHT move, the player was at cols 33-35. The blue bar was at cols 36-39. After moving RIGHT, the player is at cols 36-38, overlapping with the bar. The bar now has two segments at cols 32-35 (top and bottom).
Hmm, this is confusing. Let me look at the actual pixel counts:
- Before: blue bar had 10 pixels at cols 36-39
- After: two blue objects with 4 pixels each at cols 32-35
Wait, the coordinates don't match up. Let me re-read the output more carefully.
Before RIGHT:
- Blue: boundary=[[28, 36], [28, 39], [31, 39], [31, 36], [31, 38], [30, 39], [29, 39], [28, 38]], pixels=10 (this was the bar)
- Player: boundary=[[28, 33], [28, 35], [31, 35], [31, 33]], pixels=12
After RIGHT:
- Blue: boundary=[[28, 32], [28, 35]], pixels=4 (top bar segment)
- Blue: boundary=[[31, 32], [31, 35]], pixels=4 (bottom bar segment)  
- Player: boundary=[[28, 36], [28, 38], [31, 38], [31, 36]], pixels=12
So the player moved from cols 33-35 to cols 36-38. The blue bar was at cols 36-39 before, and now there are two blue segments at cols 32-35.
Wait, that doesn't make sense. If the player moved right from 33-35 to 36-38, and the bar was at 36-39, then the player should have overlapped with the bar. But now the bar segments are at cols 32-35, which is where the player WAS before.
Oh! I think what happened is that the player collected the part of the bar that they overlapped with. The bar was a horizontal strip, and the player moved into it. The remaining parts of the bar are now at cols 32-35 (the parts the player didn't overlap).
Actually wait - the player was at 33-35 before, and the bar was at 36-39. The player moved RIGHT to 36-38. So the player overlapped with cols 36-38 of the bar. The remaining part would be col 39. But the segmentation shows blue at cols 32-35, not col 39.
Hmm, maybe I'm misreading the boundaries. Let me look at the boundary format again. The boundary is a clockwise list of corner points. For [[28, 32], [28, 35]], this means rows 28-31 (wait, no - the boundary points are corners, not the full extent).
Actually, looking at the boundary [[28, 32], [28, 35]], this is a 2x2 object: rows 28-29, cols 32-34 (or 32-35 depending on interpretation). With 4 pixels, it's a 2x2 square.
Let me reconsider. The blue bar before was at cols 36-39 with 10 pixels. After the player moved RIGHT to overlap with it, the bar split into two 2x2 squares at cols 32-35. This doesn't make geometric sense unless the bar moved or the coordinate system is different.
Actually, I think I need to look at this differently. The player is a 4x4 object (rows 28-31, cols 36-39 based on boundary [[28, 36], [28, 38], [31, 38], [31, 36]]). Wait, that boundary suggests rows 28-31 and cols 36-38, which is 4 rows x 3 cols = 12 pixels. That matches!
So the player is 4 rows tall and 3 cols wide. The blue bar before was at cols 36-39, which is 4 cols wide. After the player moved into it, the remaining blue parts are two 2x2 squares at cols 32-35.
Wait, but cols 32-35 is to the LEFT of where the player was (cols 33-35 before). This is confusing.
Let me just look at the images. In the last image (step 171), the green player is to the right of the blue bar. The blue bar seems to have a gap in the middle where the player is. There are also blue squares above, below, and to the left of the bar.
OK, I think the key insight is:
1. The player moved RIGHT and overlapped with the blue bar
2. The part of the bar that the player overlapped with was collected/disappeared
3. The remaining parts of the bar are now visible as separate segments
The plan should be to continue collecting the remaining blue segments. Let me check what blue objects remain and plan to collect them all.
Looking at the segmentation after RIGHT:
- Blue at [[25, 45], [25, 46], [26, 46], [26, 45]] - 4 pixels (top-right blue square)
- Blue at [[28, 32], [28, 35]] - 4 pixels (top bar segment)
- Blue at [[29, 29], [29, 30], [30, 30], [30, 29]] - 4 pixels (left blue square)
- Blue at [[31, 32], [31, 35]] - 4 pixels (bottom bar segment)
- Blue at [[37, 33], [37, 34], [38, 34], [38, 33]] - 4 pixels (bottom blue square)
So there are 5 blue objects remaining. The player is at cols 36-38, rows 28-31. To collect the remaining blue objects, the player needs to move to overlap with each one.
The top bar segment is at cols 32-35, rows 28-29. The player is at cols 36-38, rows 28-31. So the player is to the right of this segment. Moving LEFT would overlap with it.
Let me try moving LEFT to collect the top bar segment.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state and try collecting remaining blue objects
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
blue_objs = [n for n in seg if n['color'] == 'b']
player = [n for n in seg if n['color'] == 'N']

print(f"Player: {player[0]['boundary'] if player else 'none'}")
print(f"Blue objects ({len(blue_objs)}):")
for b in blue_objs:
    print(f"  boundary={b['boundary']}, pixels={b['pixels']}")

# Try moving LEFT to collect the bar segments at cols 32-35
result = action(['LEFT'])
print(f"\nAfter LEFT: {last_action_result}")

seg2 = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
blue_objs2 = [n for n in seg2 if n['color'] == 'b']
player2 = [n for n in seg2 if n['color'] == 'N']
print(f"Player: {player2[0]['boundary'] if player2 else 'none'}")
print(f"Blue objects ({len(blue_objs2)}):")
for b in blue_objs2:
    print(f"  boundary={b['boundary']}, pixels={b['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
